=== PLAN ===
# COR24 Ecosystem Refactoring Saga

Migrate ~19 repos across 3 GitHub orgs into canonical sw-cor24-* names
under sw-embed. Strategy: rename/fork/clone+push, preserve history,
deprecate old repos, build portal site.

See docs/refactoring-plan.md for full details.

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-trim-emulator [legacy]: Trim sw-cor24-emulator to its scope. Work in ~/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emula...
  [x] 002-trim-assembler [legacy]: Trim sw-cor24-assembler to assembler scope. Remove emulator internals, web app, components. Keep src/assembler.rs + tests. Add path dep on ../sw-cor24-emulator. Add scripts/build.sh. Ensure tests pass.
  [x] 003-trim-web-assembler [legacy]: Trim web-sw-cor24-assembler to web app scope. Remove CLI, rust-to-cor24, emulator/assembler source. Keep src/app.rs, components/, src/wasm.rs, src/challenge.rs, styles/, index.html, Trunk.toml. Update Cargo.toml path deps to ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Update Trunk.toml public_url. Add scripts/build-pages.sh and scripts/serve.sh. Verify trunk build works.
  [x] 004-trim-rust [legacy]: Trim sw-cor24-rust to rust-to-cor24/ contents. Remove everything else. Restructure as top-level crate. Add path deps on ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Mark wasm2cor24 as deprecated. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [x] 005-fork-pcode [legacy]: Fork pv24a to sw-cor24-pcode under sw-embed. Move contents into vm/ subdir. Copy pa24r into assembler/ and pl24r into linker/. Set up Rust workspace. Add scripts/build.sh. Ensure all tests pass.
  [x] 006-fork-web-pcode [legacy]: Fork web-dv24r to web-sw-cor24-pcode under sw-embed. Update path deps to ../sw-cor24-pcode, ../sw-cor24-assembler, ../sw-cor24-emulator. Update Trunk.toml public_url. Add scripts/build-pages.sh and scripts/serve.sh.
  [x] 007-fork-pascal [legacy]: Fork p24p to sw-cor24-pascal under sw-embed. Move contents into compiler/. Copy p24c into compiler-v2/ and pr24p into runtime/. Update paths to reference sw-cor24-pcode. Add scripts/build.sh. Ensure tests pass.
  [x] 008-fork-web-pascal [legacy]: Fork web-p24c to web-sw-cor24-pascal under sw-embed. Update path deps. Update Trunk.toml public_url. Add scripts/build-pages.sh and scripts/serve.sh.
  [x] 009-fork-tinyc [legacy]: Fork tc24r to sw-cor24-tinyc under sw-embed. Update path deps to ../sw-cor24-assembler and ../sw-cor24-emulator. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [x] 010-fork-web-tinyc [legacy]: Fork web-tc24r to web-sw-cor24-tinyc under sw-embed. Update path deps and Trunk.toml. Add scripts.
  [x] 011-fork-forth [legacy]: Fork tf24a to sw-cor24-forth under sw-embed. Update path deps. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [x] 012-fork-web-forth [legacy]: Fork web-tf24a to web-sw-cor24-forth under sw-embed. Update path deps and Trunk.toml. Add scripts.
  [x] 013-fork-macrolisp [legacy]: Fork tml24c to sw-cor24-macrolisp under sw-embed. Update path deps. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [x] 014-fork-web-macrolisp [legacy]: Fork web-tml24c to web-sw-cor24-macrolisp under sw-embed. Update path deps and Trunk.toml. Add scripts.
  [ ] 015-fork-fortran [legacy]: Fork f24a to sw-cor24-fortran under sw-embed. Add scripts/build.sh. <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 016-fork-apl [legacy]: Fork a24a to sw-cor24-apl under sw-embed. Add scripts/build.sh.
  [ ] 017-deprecate-old-repos [legacy]: Add deprecation notices to all old repo READMEs. Link to new canonical repos under sw-embed.
  [ ] 018-portal-site [legacy]: Build sw-cor24-project GitHub Pages site with ISA overview, tutorials, language references, architecture diagrams, and links to all web-sw-cor24-* live demos.
  [ ] 019-verify-sweep [legacy]: Final sweep — verify all repos build/test, all demos deploy, all cross-repo links updated, all Cargo.toml package names correct, migration-status.md marked complete.

=== CURRENT STEP: 015-fork-fortran [legacy] ===
Status: pending
Description: Fork f24a to sw-cor24-fortran under sw-embed. Add scripts/build.sh.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

---
7156186 1st
Shell cwd was reset to /Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project
<system-reminder>Warning: the file exists but is shorter than the provided offset (1). The file has 1 lines.</system-reminder>

1	
2	
3	
4	
5	How hard is it to implement a Fortran (simple, early flavor) compiler targeting a custom 24-bit COR24 RISC ISA?  Was Fortran written in assembler?  Is it bootstrapped (partially written in Fortran)?  Does it make sense to implement Fortran on hardware without floating point?  Can we use a software 24-bit floating point library (I heard that this has poor accuracy).  Can we implement a 32 or 48 bit floating point software library?  Do not want/need full IEEE compliance (this is educational).  What did the early Fortrans do on 16 integer-only hardware?  I'd like to avoid using p-code/VM for this.
6	
7	
8	Thought for 28s
9	You’re asking about four linked design choices:
10	
11	Historical feasibility: how early Fortran was built and bootstrapped.
12	
13	Target fit: whether a simple early-Fortran compiler makes sense for a 24-bit integer-only COR24.
14	
15	Numeric model: integer-only, software floating point, or both.
16	
17	Code generation strategy: direct native code, not p-code.
18	
19	Bottom line
20	A small, early-flavor Fortran compiler for COR24 is quite feasible as an educational project. It is easier than a serious C compiler, and in some ways easier than Pascal, because early Fortran has a relatively simple type system, simple block structure, and a straightforward “statement → loops/branches/arithmetic” mapping. The hard part is numeric runtime support, not parsing. Historically, the first Fortran compiler was written in assembly for the IBM 704, and later versions became partially self-hosting only after the language and compiler ecosystem already existed. The original IBM 704 also had hardware floating point, which made classic early Fortran a much more natural fit there than on an integer-only machine. 
21	
22	Was Fortran originally written in assembler?
23	Yes. The original Fortran compiler lineage was built in assembly language. The Computer History Museum’s preserved FORTRAN II compiler source listing is explicitly an assembly language (SAP) listing for the IBM 704. CHM also describes the original Fortran I compiler as about 23,500 assembly-language instructions that took about 18 person-years to develop. 
24	
25	So for your kind of project, the historical precedent is firmly on the side of:
26	
27	start with assembler + a little C if useful,
28	
29	emit native machine code directly,
30	
31	keep the language subset tight.
32	
33	Was Fortran bootstrapped?
34	Not at first. The original compiler was not self-hosted. That would have been impossible at the beginning because the language had no prior compiler. Later compiler-writing systems and later language implementations did become self-hosting or partly self-hosting, but early Fortran started as hand-built assembly. 
35	
36	So your likely path is:
37	
38	Phase 1: compiler written in C and/or assembler running on host or target tools
39	
40	Phase 2: once the subset is stable, rewrite some compiler pieces in your own Fortran if you want the educational “bootstrap” milestone
41	
42	Phase 3: optionally self-host on COR24 or cross-host on another machine
43	
44	That is historically aligned and technically sane.
45	
46	How hard is it?
47	Easier parts
48	A simple Fortran I / FORTRAN II style subset is very manageable:
49	
50	fixed-format or line-based source
51	
52	implicit typing or explicit INTEGER / REAL
53	
54	assignment
55	
56	arithmetic expressions
57	
58	IF arithmetic form or simple logical comparisons
59	
60	DO loops
61	
62	GOTO
63	
64	DIMENSION
65	
66	FUNCTION / SUBROUTINE
67	
68	pass-by-reference
69	
70	static storage
71	
72	minimal I/O hooks
73	
74	That subset avoids many hard modern compiler problems:
75	
76	no complex parsing
77	
78	no structs/unions/classes
79	
80	no pointers in the C sense
81	
82	no recursion in early style
83	
84	mostly static stack frames or even static activation records
85	
86	arrays are rectangular and simple
87	
88	For COR24, the front-end is not the scary part. A simple recursive-descent parser or Pratt parser is enough.
89	
90	Harder parts
91	The real work is in:
92	
93	1. Expression lowering and temporaries
94	You only have a few general-purpose registers on COR24, so you will need a disciplined strategy for:
95	
96	accumulator-style code generation
97	
98	stack spills
99	
100	address computation for arrays
101	
102	loop induction variables
103	
104	2. Array addressing
105	Fortran arrays are central. Even an early subset wants:
106	
107	1-based indexing by default
108	
109	column-major layout if you want real Fortran flavor
110	
111	subscript arithmetic like
112	A(I,J) -> base + ((I-1) + (J-1)*LD1) * elem_size
113	
114	3. Runtime support
115	You will want library routines for:
116	
117	integer multiply/divide/mod if ISA support is limited
118	
119	software floating point
120	
121	formatted numeric output
122	
123	possibly exponentiation and elementary intrinsics later
124	
125	4. Calling convention
126	Fortran traditionally leaned heavily on:
127	
128	pass-by-reference
129	
130	hidden temporaries
131	
132	function return conventions for scalar values
133	
134	static local storage in some implementations
135	
136	no recursion assumption in early systems
137	
138	On COR24 this is all doable, but you need to design it early.
139	
140	Does Fortran make sense on hardware without floating point?
141	Yes — if you choose the subset carefully.
142	
143	But there are really three different answers.
144	
145	Option A: Integer Fortran subset
146	This is the easiest and, for your machine, the cleanest.
147	
148	Use:
149	
150	INTEGER
151	
152	integer expressions
153	
154	integer arrays
155	
156	loops
157	
158	subroutines/functions
159	
160	maybe scaled fixed-point conventions in user code
161	
162	This is historically less “classic Fortran”, but it is technically the best fit for COR24.
163	
164	Pros
165	
166	simplest compiler and runtime
167	
168	fastest generated code
169	
170	avoids the complexity of soft-float correctness
171	
172	good for teaching compiler construction
173	
174	Cons
175	
176	less aligned with the scientific-computing identity of Fortran
177	
178	many classic examples assume REAL
179	
180	Option B: Integer + software REAL
181	This is the best compromise.
182	
183	Support:
184	
185	INTEGER
186	
187	REAL
188	
189	maybe DOUBLE PRECISION later
190	
191	REAL implemented by runtime library calls
192	
193	This is historically plausible because many later and smaller machines ran Fortran with floating point handled partly or wholly in software. For example, IBM 1401 Fortran handled floating-point arithmetic interpretively because the hardware had no floating-point unit. 
194	
195	Pros
196	
197	feels like real Fortran
198	
199	supports many classic programs
200	
201	compiler remains simple if FP ops become library calls
202	
203	Cons
204	
205	runtime is much more work
206	
207	performance can be poor
208	
209	accuracy/rounding policy must be defined
210	
211	Option C: Full “real-first” Fortran
212	I would not start here on COR24.
213	
214	This would mean prioritizing REAL semantics, libraries, formatted I/O, numeric intrinsics, and maybe DOUBLE PRECISION.
215	
216	Pros
217	
218	closest to historical Fortran usage
219	
220	Cons
221	
222	runtime dominates the project
223	
224	harder to debug than the compiler itself
225	
226	easy to get bogged down
227	
228	What did early Fortrans do on integer-only hardware?
229	Not all early Fortran targets had the luxury of the IBM 704’s hardware floating point. IBM’s 1401 Fortran is a good example: the compiler ran in very small memory, generated machine code, but floating-point calculations were interpreted / handled via an interpreter-style mechanism rather than native FP hardware instructions. There were also reduced or translated Fortran systems such as FOR TRANSIT for smaller machines like the IBM 650, which adapted the language to less capable hardware. 
230	
231	So historically, the answer is:
232	
233	on strong hardware: compile to native code and use hardware FP
234	
235	on weaker hardware: compile what you can, and route the hard numeric parts through software support
236	
237	That is very close to what you would do on COR24.
238	
239	Should you use a software 24-bit floating-point format?
240	You can, but I would not make it your primary numeric type.
241	
242	A 24-bit total float is very cramped. If by “24-bit float” you mean a format where the entire value is 24 bits wide, accuracy will indeed be poor by modern expectations. You can still make it useful for demos, control systems, rough graphics, and educational numeric work, but it will be noticeably limited.
243	
244	The real issue is not that 24-bit floating point is impossible. It is that you have to decide what you want to optimize for:
245	
246	code size
247	
248	speed
249	
250	ease of implementation
251	
252	numerical range
253	
254	numerical precision
255	
256	With a 24-bit machine word, a 24-bit float is attractive because it is a single word and easy to pass around. But it is likely too weak for “Fortran-like expectations” unless you keep examples modest.
257	
258	Better choices: 32-bit or 48-bit software float
259	Yes, both are reasonable.
260	
261	32-bit soft float
262	This is probably the sweet spot.
263	
264	Use something roughly like:
265	
266	1 sign bit
267	
268	7–8 exponent bits
269	
270	23–24 significand bits total including hidden bit, or somewhat less if you want simpler packing
271	
272	Stored across two 24-bit words.
273	
274	Why it’s a good fit
275	
276	much better precision than 24-bit total float
277	
278	still manageable runtime complexity
279	
280	aligns with familiar single-precision expectations
281	
282	easier formatted I/O than truly exotic widths
283	
284	Cost
285	
286	all arithmetic becomes multiword
287	
288	more spills, more register pressure
289	
290	slower
291	
292	48-bit soft float
293	This is attractive if you want a “machine-native double-ish” feel on a 24-bit machine.
294	
295	Stored across two 24-bit words, which is elegant on COR24.
296	
297	You could define something like:
298	
299	1 sign
300	
301	11 exponent
302	
303	36 significand bits
304	or
305	
306	1 sign
307	
308	8 exponent
309	
310	39 significand bits
311	
312	This would give you noticeably better precision than 32-bit float, while mapping nicely onto two 24-bit words.
313	
314	Pros
315	
316	natural fit for 24-bit word pairs
317	
318	good educational story
319	
320	better precision for numeric demos
321	
322	Cons
323	
324	more work than 32-bit
325	
326	custom format means more custom tooling and test vectors
327	
328	Recommendation on numeric format
329	For your project, I would recommend:
330	
331	First release
332	INTEGER*24 as the native scalar
333	
334	optional REAL implemented as software 32-bit float
335	
336	no IEEE conformance goal
337	
338	simple rounding: round-to-nearest-even or even just round-to-nearest-away-from-zero if simpler
339	
340	define NaN/Inf behavior only if you want it; otherwise trap or saturate
341	
342	Later release
343	Add one of:
344	
345	REAL = 32-bit soft float
346	
347	DOUBLE PRECISION = 48-bit soft float
348	
349	That gives you a very nice educational progression.
350	
351	Does avoiding IEEE make sense?
352	Yes. Completely.
353	
354	For an educational compiler/runtime, you do not need:
355	
356	subnormals
357	
358	NaN payload rules
359	
360	signed zero edge-case fidelity
361	
362	every rounding mode
363	
364	all exception flags
365	
366	A practical educational soft-float library can define:
367	
368	normalized numbers plus zero
369	
370	maybe infinities, maybe not
371	
372	one rounding mode
373	
374	simple overflow/underflow handling
375	
376	That is enough to learn a lot.
377	
378	Architecture recommendation for COR24
379	Here is the design I would use.
380	
381	Language subset
382	Target roughly FORTRAN II-ish, but trimmed:
383	
384	PROGRAM
385	
386	INTEGER, optional REAL
387	
388	DIMENSION
389	
390	assignment
391	
392	arithmetic expressions
393	
394	IF
395	
396	maybe start with arithmetic IF because it maps simply
397	
398	GOTO
399	
400	DO
401	
402	SUBROUTINE
403	
404	FUNCTION
405	
406	CALL
407	
408	RETURN
409	
410	STOP
411	
412	maybe COMMON later
413	
414	no recursion
415	
416	no character type initially
417	
418	no formatted I/O beyond tiny runtime hooks
419	
420	Storage model
421	Start with:
422	
423	globals/statics in data segment
424	
425	locals in stack frame or static storage
426	
427	arrays contiguous
428	
429	pass arguments by address
430	
431	function results in a fixed return slot or registers if small
432	
433	For early Fortran flavor, it is perfectly acceptable to forbid recursion and keep activation records simple.
434	
435	Code generation
436	Skip any IR beyond a very small internal tree representation.
437	
438	Pipeline:
439	
440	tokenize
441	
442	parse statement
443	
444	build compact AST for expression
445	
446	lower directly to COR24 assembly
447	
448	let your assembler do the rest
449	
450	No need for SSA, no need for complex optimizers.
451	
452	Useful optimizations:
453	
454	constant folding
455	
456	strength reduction for small multiplies
457	
458	induction-variable simplification
459	
460	reuse of loaded addresses
461	
462	simple common-subexpression elimination only if it is easy
463	
464	Floating-point implementation strategy
465	Do not bake floating-point logic into the code generator at first.
466	
467	Instead, compile:
468	
469	R = A + B into a call like __f32_add
470	
471	R = A * B into __f32_mul
472	
473	comparisons into __f32_cmp
474	
475	conversion ops into __i24_to_f32, __f32_to_i24
476	
477	That keeps the compiler simple and moves complexity into the runtime library, which is the right separation.
478	
479	Should you use p-code or VM?
480	Since you explicitly do not want that, direct native code is the right choice here.
481	
482	That choice makes sense because:
483	
484	you already have an assembler and emulator
485	
486	you want to exercise the ISA directly
487	
488	Fortran statements map well to straight-line code and branches
489	
490	the runtime library can absorb the hard parts
491	
492	A VM would simplify portability, but it would hide the interesting machine-level work you seem to want.
493	
494	Practical recommendation
495	I would build this in four stages.
496	
497	Stage 1: Integer-only “Tiny Fortran”
498	Support:
499	
500	integer variables
501	
502	assignment
503	
504	IF
505	
506	GOTO
507	
508	DO
509	
510	simple arrays
511	
512	SUBROUTINE, FUNCTION
513	
514	This gets you a real compiler quickly.
515	
516	Stage 2: Runtime and calling convention cleanup
517	Add:
518	
519	pass-by-reference
520	
521	local variables
522	
523	better array indexing
524	
525	simple text I/O stubs
526	
527	Stage 3: Soft float
528	Add:
529	
530	REAL
531	
532	software 32-bit float library
533	
534	conversions and comparisons
535	
536	basic PRINT support for real values
537	
538	Stage 4: Optional richer numeric model
539	Add either:
540	
541	DOUBLE PRECISION as 48-bit soft float, or
542	
543	a better 32-bit implementation with improved formatting and math library
544	
545	My recommendation
546	For your specific goals, the best path is:
547

How hard is it to implement a Fortran (simple, early flavor) compiler targeting a custom 24-bit COR24 RISC ISA?  Was Fortran written in assembler?  Is it bootstrapped (partially written in Fortran)?  Does it make sense to implement Fortran on hardware without floating point?  Can we use a software 24-bit floating point library (I heard that this has poor accuracy).  Can we implement a 32 or 48 bit floating point software library?  Do not want/need full IEEE compliance (this is educational).  What did the early Fortrans do on 16 integer-only hardware?  I'd like to avoid using p-code/VM for this.


Thought for 28s
You’re asking about four linked design choices:

Historical feasibility: how early Fortran was built and bootstrapped.

Target fit: whether a simple early-Fortran compiler makes sense for a 24-bit integer-only COR24.

Numeric model: integer-only, software floating point, or both.

Code generation strategy: direct native code, not p-code.

Bottom line
A small, early-flavor Fortran compiler for COR24 is quite feasible as an educational project. It is easier than a serious C compiler, and in some ways easier than Pascal, because early Fortran has a relatively simple type system, simple block structure, and a straightforward “statement → loops/branches/arithmetic” mapping. The hard part is numeric runtime support, not parsing. Historically, the first Fortran compiler was written in assembly for the IBM 704, and later versions became partially self-hosting only after the language and compiler ecosystem already existed. The original IBM 704 also had hardware floating point, which made classic early Fortran a much more natural fit there than on an integer-only machine. 

Was Fortran originally written in assembler?
Yes. The original Fortran compiler lineage was built in assembly language. The Computer History Museum’s preserved FORTRAN II compiler source listing is explicitly an assembly language (SAP) listing for the IBM 704. CHM also describes the original Fortran I compiler as about 23,500 assembly-language instructions that took about 18 person-years to develop. 

So for your kind of project, the historical precedent is firmly on the side of:

start with assembler + a little C if useful,

emit native machine code directly,

keep the language subset tight.

Was Fortran bootstrapped?
Not at first. The original compiler was not self-hosted. That would have been impossible at the beginning because the language had no prior compiler. Later compiler-writing systems and later language implementations did become self-hosting or partly self-hosting, but early Fortran started as hand-built assembly. 

So your likely path is:

Phase 1: compiler written in C and/or assembler running on host or target tools

Phase 2: once the subset is stable, rewrite some compiler pieces in your own Fortran if you want the educational “bootstrap” milestone

Phase 3: optionally self-host on COR24 or cross-host on another machine

That is historically aligned and technically sane.

How hard is it?
Easier parts
A simple Fortran I / FORTRAN II style subset is very manageable:

fixed-format or line-based source

implicit typing or explicit INTEGER / REAL

assignment

arithmetic expressions

IF arithmetic form or simple logical comparisons

DO loops

GOTO

DIMENSION

FUNCTION / SUBROUTINE

pass-by-reference

static storage

minimal I/O hooks

That subset avoids many hard modern compiler problems:

no complex parsing

no structs/unions/classes

no pointers in the C sense

no recursion in early style

mostly static stack frames or even static activation records

arrays are rectangular and simple

For COR24, the front-end is not the scary part. A simple recursive-descent parser or Pratt parser is enough.

Harder parts
The real work is in:

1. Expression lowering and temporaries
You only have a few general-purpose registers on COR24, so you will need a disciplined strategy for:

accumulator-style code generation

stack spills

address computation for arrays

loop induction variables

2. Array addressing
Fortran arrays are central. Even an early subset wants:

1-based indexing by default

column-major layout if you want real Fortran flavor

subscript arithmetic like
A(I,J) -> base + ((I-1) + (J-1)*LD1) * elem_size

3. Runtime support
You will want library routines for:

integer multiply/divide/mod if ISA support is limited

software floating point

formatted numeric output

possibly exponentiation and elementary intrinsics later

4. Calling convention
Fortran traditionally leaned heavily on:

pass-by-reference

hidden temporaries

function return conventions for scalar values

static local storage in some implementations

no recursion assumption in early systems

On COR24 this is all doable, but you need to design it early.

Does Fortran make sense on hardware without floating point?
Yes — if you choose the subset carefully.

But there are really three different answers.

Option A: Integer Fortran subset
This is the easiest and, for your machine, the cleanest.

Use:

INTEGER

integer expressions

integer arrays

loops

subroutines/functions

maybe scaled fixed-point conventions in user code

This is historically less “classic Fortran”, but it is technically the best fit for COR24.

Pros

simplest compiler and runtime

fastest generated code

avoids the complexity of soft-float correctness

good for teaching compiler construction

Cons

less aligned with the scientific-computing identity of Fortran

many classic examples assume REAL

Option B: Integer + software REAL
This is the best compromise.

Support:

INTEGER

REAL

maybe DOUBLE PRECISION later

REAL implemented by runtime library calls

This is historically plausible because many later and smaller machines ran Fortran with floating point handled partly or wholly in software. For example, IBM 1401 Fortran handled floating-point arithmetic interpretively because the hardware had no floating-point unit. 

Pros

feels like real Fortran

supports many classic programs

compiler remains simple if FP ops become library calls

Cons

runtime is much more work

performance can be poor

accuracy/rounding policy must be defined

Option C: Full “real-first” Fortran
I would not start here on COR24.

This would mean prioritizing REAL semantics, libraries, formatted I/O, numeric intrinsics, and maybe DOUBLE PRECISION.

Pros

closest to historical Fortran usage

Cons

runtime dominates the project

harder to debug than the compiler itself

easy to get bogged down

What did early Fortrans do on integer-only hardware?
Not all early Fortran targets had the luxury of the IBM 704’s hardware floating point. IBM’s 1401 Fortran is a good example: the compiler ran in very small memory, generated machine code, but floating-point calculations were interpreted / handled via an interpreter-style mechanism rather than native FP hardware instructions. There were also reduced or translated Fortran systems such as FOR TRANSIT for smaller machines like the IBM 650, which adapted the language to less capable hardware. 

So historically, the answer is:

on strong hardware: compile to native code and use hardware FP

on weaker hardware: compile what you can, and route the hard numeric parts through software support

That is very close to what you would do on COR24.

Should you use a software 24-bit floating-point format?
You can, but I would not make it your primary numeric type.

A 24-bit total float is very cramped. If by “24-bit float” you mean a format where the entire value is 24 bits wide, accuracy will indeed be poor by modern expectations. You can still make it useful for demos, control systems, rough graphics, and educational numeric work, but it will be noticeably limited.

The real issue is not that 24-bit floating point is impossible. It is that you have to decide what you want to optimize for:

code size

speed

ease of implementation

numerical range

numerical precision

With a 24-bit machine word, a 24-bit float is attractive because it is a single word and easy to pass around. But it is likely too weak for “Fortran-like expectations” unless you keep examples modest.

Better choices: 32-bit or 48-bit software float
Yes, both are reasonable.

32-bit soft float
This is probably the sweet spot.

Use something roughly like:

1 sign bit

7–8 exponent bits

23–24 significand bits total including hidden bit, or somewhat less if you want simpler packing

Stored across two 24-bit words.

Why it’s a good fit

much better precision than 24-bit total float

still manageable runtime complexity

aligns with familiar single-precision expectations

easier formatted I/O than truly exotic widths

Cost

all arithmetic becomes multiword

more spills, more register pressure

slower

48-bit soft float
This is attractive if you want a “machine-native double-ish” feel on a 24-bit machine.

Stored across two 24-bit words, which is elegant on COR24.

You could define something like:

1 sign

11 exponent

36 significand bits
or

1 sign

8 exponent

39 significand bits

This would give you noticeably better precision than 32-bit float, while mapping nicely onto two 24-bit words.

Pros

natural fit for 24-bit word pairs

good educational story

better precision for numeric demos

Cons

more work than 32-bit

custom format means more custom tooling and test vectors

Recommendation on numeric format
For your project, I would recommend:

First release
INTEGER*24 as the native scalar

optional REAL implemented as software 32-bit float

no IEEE conformance goal

simple rounding: round-to-nearest-even or even just round-to-nearest-away-from-zero if simpler

define NaN/Inf behavior only if you want it; otherwise trap or saturate

Later release
Add one of:

REAL = 32-bit soft float

DOUBLE PRECISION = 48-bit soft float

That gives you a very nice educational progression.

Does avoiding IEEE make sense?
Yes. Completely.

For an educational compiler/runtime, you do not need:

subnormals

NaN payload rules

signed zero edge-case fidelity

every rounding mode

all exception flags

A practical educational soft-float library can define:

normalized numbers plus zero

maybe infinities, maybe not

one rounding mode

simple overflow/underflow handling

That is enough to learn a lot.

Architecture recommendation for COR24
Here is the design I would use.

Language subset
Target roughly FORTRAN II-ish, but trimmed:

PROGRAM

INTEGER, optional REAL

DIMENSION

assignment

arithmetic expressions

IF

maybe start with arithmetic IF because it maps simply

GOTO

DO

SUBROUTINE

FUNCTION

CALL

RETURN

STOP

maybe COMMON later

no recursion

no character type initially

no formatted I/O beyond tiny runtime hooks

Storage model
Start with:

globals/statics in data segment

locals in stack frame or static storage

arrays contiguous

pass arguments by address

function results in a fixed return slot or registers if small

For early Fortran flavor, it is perfectly acceptable to forbid recursion and keep activation records simple.

Code generation
Skip any IR beyond a very small internal tree representation.

Pipeline:

tokenize

parse statement

build compact AST for expression

lower directly to COR24 assembly

let your assembler do the rest

No need for SSA, no need for complex optimizers.

Useful optimizations:

constant folding

strength reduction for small multiplies

induction-variable simplification

reuse of loaded addresses

simple common-subexpression elimination only if it is easy

Floating-point implementation strategy
Do not bake floating-point logic into the code generator at first.

Instead, compile:

R = A + B into a call like __f32_add

R = A * B into __f32_mul

comparisons into __f32_cmp

conversion ops into __i24_to_f32, __f32_to_i24

That keeps the compiler simple and moves complexity into the runtime library, which is the right separation.

Should you use p-code or VM?
Since you explicitly do not want that, direct native code is the right choice here.

That choice makes sense because:

you already have an assembler and emulator

you want to exercise the ISA directly

Fortran statements map well to straight-line code and branches

the runtime library can absorb the hard parts

A VM would simplify portability, but it would hide the interesting machine-level work you seem to want.

Practical recommendation
I would build this in four stages.

Stage 1: Integer-only “Tiny Fortran”
Support:

integer variables

assignment

IF

GOTO

DO

simple arrays

SUBROUTINE, FUNCTION

This gets you a real compiler quickly.

Stage 2: Runtime and calling convention cleanup
Add:

pass-by-reference

local variables

better array indexing

simple text I/O stubs

Stage 3: Soft float
Add:

REAL

software 32-bit float library

conversions and comparisons

basic PRINT support for real values

Stage 4: Optional richer numeric model
Add either:

DOUBLE PRECISION as 48-bit soft float, or

a better 32-bit implementation with improved formatting and math library

My recommendation
For your specific goals, the best path is:

Yes, implement a simple early Fortran compiler for COR24.

Do direct native code generation.

Start integer-only.

Then add software 32-bit float as library calls.

If you want a distinctive “24-bit machine personality,” add 48-bit software float later as your “double precision.”

That gives you:

historically grounded design

manageable implementation scope

strong educational value

no dependence on p-code or VM

Final answers, directly
How hard is it?
Moderate, and very doable as an educational compiler if you keep the subset tight. The compiler itself is not the hardest part; the numeric runtime is.

Was Fortran written in assembler?
Yes, the original compiler lineage was assembly-based. 

Was it bootstrapped in Fortran?
Not initially. Self-hosting came later in the broader compiler world; original Fortran began as assembly. 

Does Fortran make sense on integer-only hardware?
Yes, but best as either an integer subset first or with software REAL support. Smaller machines historically used software/interpreted floating-point support. 

Should you use 24-bit software float?
You can, but I would treat it as a niche option, not the main design.

Can you use 32- or 48-bit software float instead?
Yes, and that is probably the better design. For COR24, 32-bit soft float is the best first step; 48-bit soft float is a very nice later extension.

Do you need IEEE compliance?
No. For an educational system, a simplified normalized format is enough.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete implementation plan for Tiny FORTRAN for COR24 with:

syntax subset,

calling convention,

memory layout,

runtime ABI,

and a staged test suite.


Question, what FORTRAN ran on the IBM 1130 system (and IBM 1800 system)?  What did it do for floating point (16 bit systems)?


Thought for 56s
You’re asking two things:

Which FORTRAN ran on the IBM 1130 / IBM 1800

How floating point worked on those 16-bit systems

Short answer
The common language family on both machines was IBM 1130/1800 Basic FORTRAN IV. IBM’s manual says the same Basic FORTRAN IV language was supported across the 1130 Disk Monitor systems and the 1800 MPX, TSX, and Card/Paper Tape systems, each with a FORTRAN compiler that translated source into an object program for that target system.

On the IBM 1130, the standard compiler was the IBM 1130 FORTRAN Compiler, delivered as part of both the Card/Paper Tape Programming System and the Disk Monitor System. The 1130 System Summary explicitly lists the FORTRAN compiler as one of the core software components. 

On the IBM 1800, IBM used the same language family: 1130/1800 Basic FORTRAN IV. The language manual names the supported 1800 environments as MPX, TSX, and the 1800 Card/Paper Tape Programming System. 

So, in practice:

1130: IBM 1130 FORTRAN / Basic FORTRAN IV

1800: IBM 1800 FORTRAN using the same 1130/1800 Basic FORTRAN IV language

How did floating point work on a 16-bit 1130?
The IBM 1130 was a 16-bit-word machine. IBM documentation describes it as accessing a single 16-bit word per storage cycle. 

For FORTRAN, floating point was not “one machine word = one float.” Instead, IBM used multiword software floating-point formats:

standard precision: 2 words = 32 bits

extended precision: 3 words = 40 bits

The 1130 floating formats used an 8-bit biased exponent and a signed fraction; arithmetic was performed by software routines/subroutines, not by native IEEE-style hardware floating-point instructions. A preserved description of 1130 FORTRAN states that standard precision used a 24-bit significand, extended precision used a 32-bit significand, and that floating-point arithmetic was performed by software.

What about the IBM 1800?

A compatibility source describing 1130/1800-compatible arithmetic says the standard-precision operands were two words and extended-precision operands were three words, and that these formats were identical to those used in the IBM 1130 and 1800 systems.

What is clear is that 1800 FORTRAN supported floating-point work and that the 1130/1800 software ecosystem used those same 2-word and 3-word floating formats. 

What this means for your COR24 project
The useful historical lesson is:

a 16-bit machine did not force a 16-bit float

FORTRAN on small machines commonly used software floating point in multiword formats

a practical design was:

native integer machine

software float library

compiler lowering + - * / on REAL to runtime calls

That is very close to what you were considering for COR24.

Best historical takeaway
For your purposes, the 1130/1800 story argues strongly for this design:

keep native integers as the “real machine type”

define REAL as a software format larger than one machine word

use 2-word standard precision first

optionally add a 3-word extended precision mode later

That is historically authentic enough without needing IEEE.

If you want, I can next sketch a COR24 FORTRAN numeric model modeled after the 1130:

24-bit INTEGER

48-bit REAL as 2 words

optional 72-bit EXTENDED as 3 words

# sw-cor24-fortran

Fortran compiler for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA (future/research).

Part of the [COR24 ecosystem](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project).

## Status

**Research phase** — this repository contains design notes and research
on implementing an early-flavor Fortran compiler targeting COR24.
No compiler code exists yet.

## Design Direction

- Target a FORTRAN II-ish subset (integer-first, optional software REAL)
- Direct native COR24 code generation (no p-code/VM)
- Software floating point via runtime library calls
- Inspired by IBM 1130/1800 FORTRAN approach to integer-only hardware

See `docs/research.txt` for detailed design research and historical context.

## Repository Structure

```
docs/           Design research and notes
scripts/        Build scripts (placeholder)
```

## Related Repositories

| Repository | Description |
|---|---|
| [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) | COR24 emulator + ISA |
| [sw-cor24-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-assembler) | COR24 assembler |
| [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) | Ecosystem hub |

## License

See repository for license details.
